title: Prisoner Problem (2 colors)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: 100 prisoners are each given a hat (red or blue). Each prisoner can see all OTHER prisoners' hats but not their own. They are called out one at a time in random order to guess their own hat color. Each guess is heard by all others.
rules: Prisoners can plan a strategy together before any hats are placed.
Once hats are placed, no communication is allowed -- only the publicly-spoken guesses.
Each prisoner must guess 'red' or 'blue' (no abstaining).
If a prisoner guesses correctly, they're freed. If wrong, they're executed.
The order of calling is random and unknown in advance.
setup.question: Design a strategy that maximizes the GUARANTEED number of saved prisoners.

learningObjectives: Recognize that one prisoner can SACRIFICE certainty to broadcast a parity (mod-2) bit.
Understand how subsequent prisoners decode their own hat color using the parity announcement plus what they observe.
Generalize the strategy from 2 colors (mod 2) to N colors (mod N).

stages.label: Worst-case naive bound
stages.prompt: Without coordination, what's the worst-case number of saved prisoners?

options.text: Each prisoner has a 50% chance, so on average 50 are saved -- but worst case could be 0.
0 -- they can't see their own hat.

stages.explanation: Independent guessing yields 50% expected, but no guarantee. We need a strategy that GUARANTEES the maximum -- that's where modular arithmetic comes in.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Modular arithmetic: the first prisoner announces the PARITY of the red hats they see. This gives everyone else a mod-2 reference they can use to deduce their own.
Backward induction over the call order.

stages.explanation: The first prisoner sacrifices certainty (their own guess is 50/50 because they don't know their own hat). But by encoding the PARITY of red hats they see, they broadcast a mod-2 invariant that lets everyone else figure out their own hat with certainty.

stages.label: First prisoner's encoding
stages.prompt: Prisoner 1 (the first called) counts the red hats among the OTHER 99. The agreed convention: announce 'red' if odd, 'blue' if even. What information does this convey?

options.text: The PARITY of red hats among the 99 others -- which lets everyone else deduce their own hat color, given they each know what they see.
The first prisoner's actual hat color.

stages.explanation: Prisoner 1's announcement encodes the parity of the OTHER 99 reds. Each subsequent prisoner i knows: parity of (other 99 reds) AND can SEE 98 of the others' hats (everyone except prisoner 1 and themselves -- wait, actually they can see prisoner 1's hat too). So prisoner i sees 98 others (excluding themselves), counts the reds among them, and combined with the announcement deduces their own.

stages.label: Decoding by a later prisoner
stages.prompt: Prisoner 1 announces 'red' = parity of red hats in P2..P100 is ODD. Prisoner 2 sees P1, P3, P4, ..., P100. Prisoner 2 computes: parity of red hats in P3..P100. How does P2 figure out their own hat?

options.text: P2's hat parity = (announced parity for P2..P100) XOR (parity of P3..P100, which P2 sees). If they match, P2's hat is BLUE (0); if they differ, P2's hat is RED (1).
P2 needs another announcement to determine their own.
P2 can't determine theirs -- only P3 onward can.

stages.explanation: The announcement gives parity of {P2 + P3 + ... + P100}. P2 sees and counts {P3 + ... + P100}. The XOR (i.e., announced parity minus seen parity, mod 2) is P2's own hat. P3 onwards: same idea, but they also use the spoken guesses of earlier prisoners (which are correct) to update.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: How many prisoners are GUARANTEED to be saved?

options.text: At least 99. Prisoner 1 has a 50% chance; everyone else can deduce theirs with certainty using the announced parity.
50 in expectation but 0 in worst case.

stages.explanation: Prisoner 1 has 50% (might announce 'red' when his own is blue, etc.). The other 99 each combine prisoner 1's announcement with what they see to deduce their own with certainty. Worst case: 99 saved.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: How does the strategy generalize to 3 colors? (Hint: parity becomes residue mod 3.)
What if prisoners are called in a known order? Does the answer change?
Why is exactly ONE prisoner sacrificing necessary -- could you sacrifice 'less'?
